findings.evidence_dynamic: REST API endpoint /api/Products/{id} returns different user-specific content for different IDs without authorization check
findings.remediation: To remediate the idor vulnerability, ensure that proper authorization checks are in place for the `/api/Products/{id}` endpoint. A concrete code-level fix example would be to add a middleware function that checks if the authenticated user has access to the requested product ID, e.g., `if (authenticatedUser.id === productId) { return next(); } else { return res.status(403).send('Forbidden'); }`. Implementing role-based access control (RBAC) or attribute-based access control (ABAC) can also help restrict access to sensitive data.

findings.evidence_dynamic: Header 'Content-Security-Policy' is missing. Risk: No XSS mitigation policy — scripts from any source allowed
findings.remediation: To remediate the Content-Security-Policy header vulnerability, add the 'Content-Security-Policy' header to your server's response with a suitable policy, such as 'default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' https://trusted-scripts.com;'. For example, in a Node.js Express server, you can set this header using `res.setHeader('Content-Security-Policy', "default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' https://trusted-scripts.com;")`. This will mitigate XSS risks by only allowing scripts from trusted sources to be executed.

findings.evidence_dynamic: Header 'Strict-Transport-Security' is missing. Risk: HTTP downgrade attacks — HTTPS not enforced
findings.remediation: To remediate the missing Strict-Transport-Security header, set the `Strict-Transport-Security` header in your server configuration to enforce HTTPS. For example, in Node.js with Express, you can add the header using `app.use helmet()` and then `app.use(helmet.hsts())`. Alternatively, you can manually set the header with `res.setHeader('Strict-Transport-Security', 'max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains')`.

findings.evidence_dynamic: Header 'Referrer-Policy' is missing. Risk: URL data leaked to third parties via Referer header
findings.remediation: To remediate the Referrer-Policy header vulnerability, set the 'Referrer-Policy' header in your server configuration to a suitable value such as 'same-origin' or 'no-referrer'. This can be achieved in a Node.js application using Express.js by adding the line `app.use((req, res, next) => { res.setHeader("Referrer-Policy", "same-origin"); next(); });`. By doing so, you prevent URL data from being leaked to third parties via the Referer header.

findings.evidence_dynamic: Header 'Permissions-Policy' is missing. Risk: No browser feature restrictions defined
findings.remediation: To remediate the Permissions-Policy header vulnerability, add the 'Permissions-Policy' header to the server's response with the desired permissions, such as 'Permissions-Policy: geolocation=(), microphone=()'. This can be achieved in a Node.js server using the Express framework by adding the following code: `res.setHeader("Permissions-Policy", "geolocation=(), microphone=()");`. By doing so, you define restrictions on browser features, enhancing the security of your application.
